cell_specs.implementation_plan: Environment setup and centralized imports. (1) Wrap pip installs in a try/except: attempt 'import networkx, matplotlib, ipywidgets'; on ImportError run '!pip install -q networkx matplotlib ipywidgets' via get_ipython().system, then re-import. (2) Import standard libraries: heapq, math, time, random. (3) Import networkx as nx, matplotlib.pyplot as plt, ipywidgets as widgets plus 'from ipywidgets import interact, IntSlider, Dropdown, Checkbox, Play'. (4) Enable inline plotting via get_ipython().run_line_magic('matplotlib', 'inline') guarded by 'if get_ipython() is not None'. (5) Seed random with a fixed value (random.seed(42)) for reproducible timing graphs. (6) Print a one-line confirmation including the resolved networkx and matplotlib versions.
state_variables: heapq, math, time, random (stdlib modules in global namespace)
error_handling: Catch ImportError around the initial imports and trigger pip install before re-importing.
Guard the matplotlib inline magic with 'if get_ipython() is not None' so the cell does not crash outside IPython.
If pip install fails, print an actionable message naming the missing package rather than letting a raw traceback propagate.
test_checks: assert all(m in globals() for m in ['heapq','math','time','random','nx','plt','widgets'])
Print 'Setup OK - networkx', nx.__version__ to confirm successful import.
Confirm random.seed(42) was applied (documented) and no exception was raised.

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Build the lecture example graph and the shared drawing primitive. (1) Define example_graph as a networkx.DiGraph (or plain adjacency dict {u: [(v, w), ...]} if nx unavailable) with vertices s,t,x,y,z and the CLRS slide-3 edges: s->t(10), s->y(5), t->x(1), t->y(2), y->t(3), y->x(9), y->z(2), x->z(4), z->x(6), z->s(7). (2) Define POS, a fixed dict of (x,y) coordinates for s,t,x,y,z so layout is stable across redraws. (3) Implement draw_graph(graph, pos, dist=None, pi=None, S=None, u=None, frontier=None, title=None, ax=None): create or reuse an axis; color nodes by state - S members black (white font), active vertex u gray, others (Q) white with black border; draw all edges light gray; if pi is given, highlight predecessor (tree) edges in a bold color; annotate each node with its current dist ('INF' for math.inf); draw edge-weight labels; set the title/caption. (4) Render the initial example graph once to verify the helper.
function_signatures: draw_graph(graph, pos, dist=None, pi=None, S=None, u=None, frontier=None, title=None, ax=None) -> matplotlib.axes.Axes
error_handling: If a node in S/u/frontier is absent from graph, skip it defensively rather than raising KeyError.
Render math.inf distances as the string 'INF' to avoid matplotlib label errors.
If networkx is unavailable, fall back to manual matplotlib drawing using POS and the adjacency dict.
When ax is None create a fresh figure/axis; when provided, ax.clear() so animation redraws do not stack artists.
test_checks: assert set of example_graph nodes == {'s','t','x','y','z'}
assert example_graph edge count == 10 (sum of adjacency list lengths == 10 in dict form)
assert set(POS.keys()) == set of example_graph nodes
Call draw_graph once and assert it returns an Axes without raising.

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Implement the two atomic shortest-path primitives as pure, reusable functions. (1) initialize_single_source(graph, s): return (dist, pi) where dist maps every vertex to math.inf except dist[s]=0, and pi maps every vertex to None; iterate vertices via a representation-agnostic helper get_vertices(graph). (2) relax(u, v, w, dist, pi): if dist[u] + w < dist[v], set dist[v] = dist[u] + w, pi[v] = u, return True; else return False (inf + finite == inf in Python, which is safe). (3) Print a sanity check: initialize on example_graph from s and show dist with s=0 and all others INF.
function_signatures: relax(u, v, w, dist, pi) -> bool
state_variables: dist (dict vertex -> float distance estimate, sanity-check instance)
pi (dict vertex -> predecessor or None, sanity-check instance)
error_handling: Raise a clear ValueError if s is not in the graph's vertex set.
In relax, treat missing keys as a programming error - assert u in dist and v in dist with an explanatory message.
relax itself is weight-sign agnostic; non-negativity is enforced by algorithm assumptions and exercised in C14 (documented here).
test_checks: d, p = initialize_single_source(example_graph, 's'); assert d['s'] == 0 and all(d[v] == math.inf for v in get_vertices(example_graph) if v != 's')
assert all(p[v] is None for v in get_vertices(example_graph))
dist={'a':0,'b':math.inf}; pi={'a':None,'b':None}; assert relax('a','b',3,dist,pi) is True and dist['b'] == 3 and pi['b'] == 'a'
assert relax('a','b',5,dist,pi) is False (no update when 0+5 > existing 3)

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Interactive single-edge relaxation sanity check. (1) Build labeled edge options 'u->v (w=W)' mapped to (u, v, w) tuples from example_graph for a Dropdown. (2) Define relaxation_demo(edge_label, ud, vd): look up (u, v, w); build temporary dist={u: ud, v: vd}, pi={v: None}; capture before value vd; call relax(u, v, w, dist, pi); print before/after v.d, whether the condition ud + w < vd held, and the resulting predecessor. (3) Wire with interact: Dropdown(options=edge_labels), IntSlider(min=0, max=20) for u.d, IntSlider(min=0, max=30) for v.d. Keep output as plain printed text for Colab reliability.
function_signatures: relaxation_demo(edge_label: str, ud: int, vd: int) -> None
state_variables: edge_options (dict label -> (u, v, w)) derived from example_graph
error_handling: If get_ipython() is None or ipywidgets unavailable, fall back to a single non-interactive call with default arguments plus a printed note.
Sliders enforce min=0 so values stay non-negative, within Dijkstra's assumptions.
Support a large sentinel value for v.d to mimic an unrelaxed vertex and label it clearly.
test_checks: Call relaxation_demo(first_edge_label, 0, 100) and confirm relaxation occurs (after < before).
Call relaxation_demo(first_edge_label, 100, 0) and confirm no relaxation (after == before).
assert len(edge_options) == 10 (all example-graph edges available).

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Implement traced Dijkstra over a binary heap. (1) dijkstra_with_trace(graph, s): dist, pi = initialize_single_source(graph, s); S=set(); steps=[]; pq=[(0, s)]. Use lazy deletion since heapq lacks decrease-key. (2) Main loop: while pq, heappop (d, u); if u in S continue; add u to S; record a snapshot AFTER extract-min with caption f'Extract-Min: u = {u} (d={d})'. (3) For each neighbor v with weight w of u: did = relax(u, v, w, dist, pi); if did: heappush(pq, (dist[v], v)) and record a snapshot caption f'Relax({u}, {v}): {v}.d = {dist[v]}'. (4) Each snapshot is {'S': set(S), 'u': u, 'dist': dict(dist), 'pi': dict(pi), 'frontier': sorted vertices in pq not in S, 'caption': caption}; deep-copy per snapshot. (5) Return (steps, dist, pi). (6) Run on example_graph, print final dist and assert it equals the answer key.
state_variables: trace_steps (list of snapshot dicts for the example graph)
final_dist, final_pi (dicts from the example-graph run)
pq (heap list of (distance, vertex)), S (set of finalized vertices)
error_handling: Use lazy deletion ('if u in S: continue') to handle stale/duplicate heap entries safely instead of a non-existent decrease-key.
Deep-copy (dict(dist), dict(pi), set(S)) into every snapshot so animation frames are immutable and independent.
Raise ValueError if s not in graph before starting.
Heap tuples are (distance, vertex) so equal distances tie-break deterministically on the comparable vertex label and never raise.
test_checks: steps, d, _ = dijkstra_with_trace(example_graph, 's'); assert d == {'s':0,'y':5,'t':8,'z':7,'x':9}
assert len(steps) > 0 and all({'S','u','dist','pi','frontier','caption'} <= set(snap) for snap in steps)
assert steps[0]['u'] == 's' (source extracted first)
assert len(steps[-1]['S']) == 5 (all vertices finalized by the end)

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Animated scrubber over the example-graph trace. (1) Define show_step(i): fetch snap = trace_steps[i], ax.clear(), call draw_graph(example_graph, POS, dist=snap['dist'], pi=snap['pi'], S=snap['S'], u=snap['u'], frontier=snap['frontier'], title=f"Step {i+1}/{len(trace_steps)}: {snap['caption']}"), then display. (2) Wire interact(show_step, i=IntSlider(min=0, max=len(trace_steps)-1, step=1, value=0)); optionally link a Play widget to the same IntSlider via widgets.jslink for auto-advance. (3) Create fig, ax once and reuse them so Colab is not flooded with images.
state_variables: fig, ax (single reused matplotlib figure/axis for redraws)
error_handling: Clamp i into [0, len(trace_steps)-1] before indexing.
If trace_steps is empty, print a message to run C08 first instead of indexing.
ax.clear() each frame to prevent artist accumulation; if ipywidgets unavailable, render the final step statically with a note.
test_checks: assert len(trace_steps) >= 1 before building the slider.
Call show_step(0) and show_step(len(trace_steps)-1) and assert neither raises.
assert step_slider.max == len(trace_steps) - 1

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Practice graph with predict-then-verify flow. (1) Build practice_graph (slide-4 vertices s,a,b,c,d,e) as a DiGraph/adjacency dict with weights chosen so the answer key holds: final distances a=8, b=5, c=9, d=7, e=10 from source s. Define PRACTICE_POS fixed coordinates. (2) Define EXPECTED_PRACTICE = {'s':0,'a':8,'b':5,'c':9,'d':7,'e':10}. (3) practice_steps, practice_dist, _ = dijkstra_with_trace(practice_graph, 's'). (4) Build show_practice_step(i) analogous to C09 over practice_steps reusing draw_graph. (5) Add a reveal_answers(reveal) Checkbox handler: unchecked prompts the student to predict; checked prints practice_dist alongside EXPECTED_PRACTICE with a per-vertex match table. Wire interact(reveal=Checkbox(value=False)) and an IntSlider over practice steps.
error_handling: Assert at build time that practice_graph reproduces EXPECTED_PRACTICE; raise AssertionError with a diff if weights were mis-entered.
Clamp slider index; guard empty practice_steps.
Fall back to static final-state render plus printed answer key if ipywidgets unavailable.
test_checks: assert practice_dist == EXPECTED_PRACTICE
assert set of practice_graph nodes == {'s','a','b','c','d','e'}
Call show_practice_step(0) and reveal_answers(True) and assert neither raises.

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Empirical array-vs-heap timing. (1) dijkstra_array(graph, s): dist, pi = initialize_single_source; S=set(); while |S| < |V|: linear scan over V-S to find u with minimum dist (Extract-Min in O(V)); if the min is math.inf break (unreachable remainder); add u to S; relax all neighbors. Return dist. (2) dijkstra_heap(graph, s): same core as C08 without snapshot recording, returning only dist. (3) make_random_graph(n, density, max_w, seed): DiGraph on n vertices with random non-negative integer weights in [1, max_w], adding a random spanning path first to guarantee reachability from source 0. (4) For n in a small list (e.g., [10,25,50,100,150]): build a graph, time each algorithm with time.perf_counter over a few repeats, record averages. (5) Print a timing table and plot runtime vs n for array (O(V^2)) and heap (O(E lg V)) on shared matplotlib axes with a legend. (6) Optional IntSlider to set max n / density and re-run.
function_signatures: make_random_graph(n: int, density: float, max_w: int = 20, seed: int | None = None) -> graph
time_algorithm(fn, graph, s, repeats: int = 3) -> float
state_variables: sizes (list of vertex counts to benchmark)
error_handling: Keep default sizes small and density moderate so Colab stays responsive; cap n to avoid runaway O(V^2) cost.
Add a spanning path so the source reaches all vertices, preventing timings dominated by early INF breaks.
Seed graph generation for reproducibility and pass the SAME generated graph to both algorithms for a fair comparison.
Wrap each timed run in try/except to skip a failing size rather than aborting the whole sweep.
test_checks: g = make_random_graph(20, 0.3, seed=1); assert dijkstra_array(g, 0) == dijkstra_heap(g, 0) (algorithms agree on a shared graph)
assert all(timings[n]['array'] >= 0 and timings[n]['heap'] >= 0 for n in sizes)
assert dijkstra_heap(example_graph, 's') == {'s':0,'y':5,'t':8,'z':7,'x':9} (non-tracing heap matches the traced result).

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Negative-edge counterexample. (1) Build neg_graph, a small cycle-free graph where a negative edge makes Dijkstra err (classic: s->u(1), s->v(2), v->u(-2): true dist[u]=0 via s->v->u, but Dijkstra finalizes u at 1). Hard-code with a comment proving the discrepancy and define NEG_POS. (2) bellman_ford(graph, s): V-1 relaxation passes over all edges returning correct shortest distances as ground truth. (3) Run dijkstra_heap and bellman_ford on neg_graph; print a side-by-side table of vertex, Dijkstra dist, true dist, and a 'WRONG' flag where they differ. (4) Draw neg_graph with draw_graph highlighting the negative edge. (5) Optional compare_on_weight(w) with an IntSlider on the offending edge weight from positive down through negative so students see exactly when the answer breaks.
error_handling: bellman_ford runs a V-th pass and warns if any edge still relaxes (negative cycle), keeping the demo graph cycle-free by construction.
When the slider sets the offending edge non-negative, the demo shows Dijkstra and Bellman-Ford agreeing, making the breaking point explicit.
Reuse draw_graph defensively; the negative-weight label is finite and must render (not 'INF').
test_checks: true = bellman_ford(neg_graph, 's'); dij = dijkstra_heap(neg_graph, 's'); assert any(true[v] != dij[v] for v in true) (the counterexample exhibits a real discrepancy).
With the offending edge set non-negative, assert bellman_ford and dijkstra_heap agree (failure is specifically due to negativity).
A V-th Bellman-Ford pass produces no further relaxation (no negative cycle).

cross_cell_invariants: All cells run strictly top-to-bottom; every function a cell uses is defined in an earlier cell (C02 imports, C04 graph+draw_graph, C06 primitives, C08 traced Dijkstra) with no forward references.
Graphs are represented consistently (networkx.DiGraph or fallback adjacency dict {u: [(v, w)]}) and accessed only through representation-agnostic helpers get_vertices(graph) and neighbors(graph, u).
Distance maps use math.inf for unreached vertices and 0 for the source; predecessor maps use None for no predecessor; draw_graph renders math.inf as 'INF'.
draw_graph is the single visualization primitive reused by C09, C10, and C14; its node-coloring contract is fixed: S=black, active u=gray, Q/frontier=white.
Every trace snapshot is an independent deep copy (set(S), dict(dist), dict(pi)) so scrubbing forward/backward never mutates earlier frames.
The example-graph answer key {s:0,y:5,t:8,z:7,x:9} and practice-graph answer key {s:0,a:8,b:5,c:9,d:7,e:10} are the authoritative correctness targets reproduced by C08/C10.
All graphs except the C14 counterexample have non-negative edge weights, satisfying Dijkstra's correctness precondition.
ipywidgets is the only interaction library; every interactive cell (C07, C09, C10, C12, C14) degrades to a static default render when get_ipython() is None or ipywidgets is unavailable.
assumptions: The target Colab/IPython runtime supports ipywidgets interaction; static fallbacks are provided for non-interactive execution (CI or plain Jupyter without widget support).
networkx and matplotlib are importable or pip-installable in C02; draw_graph falls back to manual matplotlib drawing on the fixed position dictionaries if networkx is unavailable.
The edge weights of the example and practice graphs are those that yield the answer keys from 01_concepts.json; build functions assert the keys at construction time to catch transcription errors.
C12 uses small, Colab-friendly graph sizes and a fixed random seed so the O(V^2) vs O(E lg V) trend is visible and reproducible without long runtimes.
The negative-edge graph in C14 is deliberately cycle-free so Bellman-Ford yields a well-defined ground truth for comparison.
Heap entries are (distance, vertex) tuples with deterministic vertex tie-breaking, and stale entries are handled by lazy deletion since heapq provides no decrease-key operation.
